Migrated to DB-driven loading: closed_vocab.init now runs SELECT id, name FROM T_WC_TMDB_GENRE for canonicals and SELECT id, name FROM T_WC_TMDB_GENRE_LANG for multilingual aliases (currently French; auto-extends to any LANG inserted). JSON Sci-Fi alias targets updated to point at the DB canonical Science Fiction; sci-fi added explicitly to prevent fuzzy mismatch with Sci-Fi & Fantasy. Single source of truth for canonicals = T_WC_TMDB_GENRE / T_WC_TMDB_GENRE_LANG

canonical_source_detail: SELECT DISTINCT CREW_DEPARTMENT FROM T_WC_T2S_PERSON_MOVIE WHERE CREW_DEPARTMENT IS NOT NULL AND CREW_DEPARTMENT NOT IN ('Actors', 'Acting') UNION SELECT DISTINCT CREW_DEPARTMENT FROM T_WC_T2S_PERSON_SERIE WHERE CREW_DEPARTMENT IS NOT NULL AND CREW_DEPARTMENT NOT IN ('Actors', 'Acting') UNION SELECT DISTINCT KNOWN_FOR_DEPARTMENT FROM T_WC_T2S_PERSON WHERE KNOWN_FOR_DEPARTMENT IS NOT NULL AND KNOWN_FOR_DEPARTMENT NOT IN ('Actors', 'Acting')
notes: CREW-ONLY scope: 'Actors' and 'Acting' are excluded from canonicals in all three UNIONed source columns. Cast / acting credits route via CREDIT_TYPE='cast' inline at the text-to-SQL step and never produce a Department_name placeholder. DB-driven canonicals via UNION across CREW_DEPARTMENT (movies + series) and KNOWN_FOR_DEPARTMENT (persons); same vocabulary, two different columns chosen by question intent (person-search -> KNOWN_FOR_DEPARTMENT, crew-of-content -> CREW_DEPARTMENT, with CREDIT_TYPE='crew' enforced on the same WHERE clause). Resolver = closed_vocab.resolve('Department_name', ...) loaded by closed_vocab.init() at startup. Entity branch in entity.py shares dispatch with Status_name / Serie_type (substitutes canonical string with single quotes). data/entity_extraction.md: dedicated Department_name section + boundaries explicitly excluding Acting/cast roles; 'List actors' / 'Actresses in The Big Lebowski' negative examples added; 'Actors born in 1962' example reverted to NOT extract Department_name; 'Directors who died in 1980' still extracts Department_name=Directors. data/text_to_sql.md: Person Search section now mandates CREDIT_TYPE='crew' alongside CREW_DEPARTMENT={{Department_nameN}}, plus an explicit actor/cast rule directing the LLM to CREDIT_TYPE='cast' for any actor/actress/cast question. Aliases in data/closed_vocabularies.json cover crew plural forms (directors, writers, editors, cinematographers, producers, creators), English synonyms (DP, DOP, VFX, screenwriting, production design, costume, makeup), and French equivalents (réalisateurs, scénaristes, monteurs, producteurs, créateurs, chef opérateur, directeur de la photographie, etc.); all Acting-mapped aliases (actor, actors, actress, actresses, acteur(s), actrice(s), cast) were removed. Canonical values: Art, Camera, Costume & Make-Up, Crew, Directing, Editing, Lighting, Production, Sound, Visual Effects, Writing, Creator (series only). Acting is no longer in the vocabulary. No T_WC_T2S_DEPARTMENT_LANG companion table; multilingual aliases live in JSON only

canonical_source_detail: SELECT DISTINCT ASPECT_RATIO FROM T_WC_T2S_MOVIE WHERE ASPECT_RATIO IS NOT NULL AND ASPECT_RATIO REGEXP '^[0-9]+,[0-9]+$'
notes: COMMA-DECIMAL CANONICALS ONLY: T_WC_T2S_MOVIE.ASPECT_RATIO is stored in French decimal notation (comma not dot, e.g. '1,33', '2,35'). The loader query filters DB values with REGEXP '^[0-9]+,[0-9]+$' so noisy DB variants ('4:3', '4/3', '16:9', '1:33', '235:1', '12,35' typo, etc.) are excluded from the canonical map. Those variants instead fall through to the alias layer, which remaps each common surface form to its dominant comma-decimal canonical. Tradeoff: the few hundred rows tagged with non-canonical surface forms (24 rows '4:3', 53 rows '4/3', 45 rows '16:9', 82 rows '16/9') are NOT matched when the user queries that exact form — instead the resolver returns the dominant comma-decimal form (1,33 has 3661 rows, 1,78 has 483 rows, etc.). Aliases in data/closed_vocabularies.json all target comma-decimal: named conventions (academy / academy ratio / academy aperture -> 1,37; fullscreen / full screen -> 1,33; european widescreen -> 1,66; widescreen / flat -> 1,85; modern anamorphic -> 2,39), width:height notations (4:3 / 4/3 / 4x3 -> 1,33; 16:9 / 16/9 / 16x9 -> 1,78; 21:9 -> 2,39), N.NN:1 / N,NN:1 / N:NN dot-and-colon variants (1.33:1 / 1,33:1 / 1:33 -> 1,33; same shape for 1,37 / 1,66 / 1,78 / 1,85 / 2,35 / 2,39 / 2,40), and bare dot-decimal user input (1.33 / 1.85 / 2.35 -> comma equivalents). 'anamorphic' / 'scope' / 'cinemascope' deliberately removed from Aspect_ratio aliases — they belong to Technical_format and would otherwise collide. No collapse of 2,40 into 2,39 (each maps to its own canonical). Canonical decimal values now produced: 1,33 / 1,37 / 1,66 / 1,78 / 1,85 / 2,35 / 2,39 / 2,40 / 2,55 / etc (any value matching the regex, ~30+ ratios). No T_WC_T2S_ASPECT_RATIO_LANG companion table; aliases live in JSON only
